33: The Chronicler names Saul’s sons as Jonathan, Malchishua, Abinadab, and ʾeš baʿal (Eshbaal, ‘man of Baal’); Jonathan’s son is named mᵉrîḇ baʿal (Merib-baal, ‘Baal contends/pleads’). These are the original theophoric names. The parallel passages in Samuel use ʾîšbōšet (Ishbosheth, ‘man of shame’) and mᵉpîḇōšet (Mephibosheth, ‘from the mouth of shame’) — systematic substitutions of bōšet (shame, disgrace) for baʿal. This scribal practice of replacing Baal-element theophoric names with bōšet is attested across Samuel and reflects the later theological aversion to preserving Baal-names even in personal names from the Saul-David period. The Chronicler preserves the older forms: the theophoric baʿal in these pre-monarchic and early monarchic personal names did not necessarily indicate Baal-worship — baʿal as a common noun means ‘lord’ or ‘master’ and was sometimes used of YHWH (Hosea 2:16: tiqrᵉʾî ʾîšî wᵉlōʾ tiqrᵉʾî lî ʿôḏ baʿlî, ‘you will call me “My Husband” and no longer call me “My Baal”’ — YHWH himself once called baʿal). The Samuel substitutions are a later editorial censorship; the Chronicler’s retention of Eshbaal and Merib-baal reflects either access to earlier sources or a different editorial policy. The LXX often mediates between both traditions.
1: Benjamin’s sons in 1 Chronicles 8 (Bela, Ashbel, Aharah, Nohah, Rapha) differ from Genesis 46:21 (ten sons including Muppim, Huppim, Ard) and Numbers 26:38–40 (five with Ahiram, Shupham, Hupham). “Aharah” may be a scribal variant of “Ahiram” (transposition of consonants); “Nohah” and “Rapha” have no Pentateuchal parallel. The Chronicler is using a distinct Benjaminite source for 1 Chronicles 8 — possibly a tribal register compiled independently of Genesis and Numbers — resulting in the two partially overlapping Benjaminite genealogies of 1 Chronicles 7 and 1 Chronicles 8.
2: The ordinal designations (firstborn, second, third, fourth, fifth) for Benjamin’s five sons establish primogeniture order for the tribal genealogy. The ordinal series is unusual: the Chronicler normally lists names without rank, but here the ranking parallels the format used in Numbers 26:38–40 where Benjamin’s sons are listed as “after their families.” The ordinal sequence anchors Bela as the genealogically primary line, which explains why Bela’s descendants receive the most extensive treatment in what follows.
3: Bela’s first three sons (Addar, Gera, Abihud) represent the first generation of the expansion. Gera is a recurring Benjaminite name: Ehud’s father (Judges 3:15), Shimei’s father (2 Samuel 16:5), and here a clan in Bela’s line. The name’s repetition across generations and genealogical texts suggests it was a prestige clan name in Benjamin, recycled through multiple generations as a way of maintaining genealogical continuity with the founding ancestor.
4: Abishua, Naaman, and Ahoah continue Bela’s list. Naaman (nōʿamān, ‘pleasantness/grace’) is notably the name of the Aramean general healed of leprosy by Elisha (2 Kings 5:1) — the same name in a Benjaminite tribal context confirms it was an Israelite as well as Aramean name. Naaman also appears in Numbers 26:40 as a Benjaminite clan, consistent with this genealogy’s overlap with Numbers.
5: Gera, Shephuphan, and Huram close Bela’s first sub-list. The second Gera in this list (v5 vs. v3) is either a second son named Gera or a textual error; the Chronicler’s tolerance for repeated personal names within the same family reflects actual ancient naming practice (naming after grandfathers). Shephuphan is a variant of Shupham/Muppim from Genesis and Numbers, confirming this list is partly parallel to and partly divergent from the Pentateuchal sources.
6: The sons of Ehud (ʾēhûḏ) — the famous left-handed judge (Judges 3:15) was a Benjaminite, son of Gera. Here Ehud’s descendants are identified as “heads of ancestral houses of the inhabitants of Geba.” Geba was a Benjaminite priestly city on the border with the Philistines (1 Samuel 13:3, 16). The exile to Manahath (wayyigᵉlûm ʾel mᵉnaḥat) is otherwise unattested; Manahath is a Calebite place in the Shephelah (1 Chronicles 2:52, 54), suggesting an intra-tribal movement rather than a foreign exile.
7: Naaman, Ahijah, and Gera; “he removed them” (hûʾ higᵉlām) — the antecedent of “he” is syntactically ambiguous in the MT. Following the MT literally, Gera of Ehud’s line is the one who “removed them” to Manahath — possibly a forced resettlement of part of the Ehudite sub-clan, perhaps for punishment or administrative reorganization. The verse then notes Gera “fathered Uzza and Ahihud” after the relocation, continuing the genealogy past the disruption. LXX and Syriac smooth the syntax differently.
8: Shaharaim (‘double dawn’ or ‘the dawn-double’) is an otherwise unattested figure whose personal story is entirely unique to Chronicles. He “fathered children in the land of Moab after he had divorced his wives Hushim and Baara.” The verb šallaḥ (to send away, Piel) is the technical term for divorce. The detail that a Benjaminite patriarch settled in Moab and started a second family there is remarkable; it creates an Israelite family tradition in Moab parallel to the Ruth narrative, though moving in the opposite direction.
9: By his new wife Hodesh (‘the new moon’ — a lunar theophoric name) Shaharaim fathers seven sons. Jobab appears elsewhere as an Edomite king (Genesis 36:33–34) and a Canaanite city-king (Joshua 11:1); the name was shared across ethnic groups in the southern Levant. Zibia (ṣibyāʾ, ‘gazelle’) is a feminine name in its more common form (2 Kings 12:1), suggesting that male and female forms of animal names were used interchangeably in the naming tradition.
10: Jeuz, Sachia, and Mirmah close Hodesh’s sons, designated “heads of ancestral houses.” Whatever the moral ambiguity of Shaharaim’s marital history, his sons by Hodesh achieve the honorific rāʾšê ʾābôt (heads of fathers’ houses) — the Chronicler records genealogical legitimacy without editorial judgment on the patriarch’s personal conduct. The genealogy reports; it does not adjudicate.
11: By his first wife Hushim, Shaharaim had also fathered Abitub and Elpaal before the divorce. Elpaal (‘God has performed’ or ‘El works’; ʾel pāʿal) becomes the primary genealogical line in vv12–18. The Chronicler tracks both sets of children from Shaharaim’s divided household, preserving the parallel branches even though they originated from different mothers in different countries.
12: Elpaal’s sons include Eber, Misham, and Shemed, with the editorial note that “Shemed built Ono and Lod with its towns.” Ono and Lod are in the Shephelah near the modern Tel Aviv coastal plain; Lod is present-day Lod/Lydda. These cities appear in Ezra 2:33 and Nehemiah 11:35 as Benjaminite post-exilic settlement towns, so Shemed’s city-founding establishes the genealogical claim to these sites that the returning community would have exercised after the Babylonian exile. The formula “with its towns” (wᵉbᵉnōtêhā) is the standard territorial formula for a city with dependent villages.
13: Beriah and Shema are “heads of ancestral houses for the inhabitants of Aijalon, who drove out the inhabitants of Gath.” The Aijalon valley was Joshua’s battlefield (Joshua 10:12); the connection to driving out Gath’s inhabitants is historically puzzling, since Gath is a major Philistine city far to the southwest. This may refer to a local Gath-enclave within Benjaminite territory (several places bore the name Gath, meaning ‘winepress’) or to a Philistine outpost in the Aijalon region. The verb bāraḥ (Hiphil: to drive out) marks this as a victory claim embedded in the genealogy.
14: Ahio, Shashak, and Jeremoth are listed without sub-grouping here — their family attribution comes in the following verses. The name Ahio (ʾaḥyô, ‘his brother’ or ‘YHWH is my brother’) is the same name as one of Abinadab’s sons who accompanied the ark-cart (2 Samuel 6:3–4); the Benjaminite context here differs from the Gittite-Judahite context there.
15: Zebadiah, Arad, and Eder are three more Benjaminite heads. Arad (ʿărāḏ) is best known as the Canaanite city in the Negev (Numbers 21:1; Joshua 12:14); its use as a personal name may reflect family tradition connected to that region, or it may be an entirely independent naming. The multiplicity of names without stories in these lists reflects the reality of genealogical records: most ancestors leave only a name, not a narrative.
16: Michael, Ishpah, and Joha are identified as “sons of Beriah” (connecting back to v13). Michael (mîkāʾēl, ‘who is like God?’) is the most theologically freighted personal name in this list; the rhetorical question embedded in the name is a devotional formula asserting YHWH’s incomparability (cf. Micah 7:18, mî ʾēl kāmôkā, ‘who is a God like you?’). The name appears for multiple individuals in Chronicles.
17: Zebadiah, Meshullam, Hizki, and Heber are “sons of Elpaal.” Meshullam (mᵉšullām, ‘rewarded/repaid’ or ‘at peace with God’) is one of the most common names in Ezra-Nehemiah, reflecting its post-exilic popularity; its appearance in this pre-exilic Benjaminite list shows the name had an older history. Hizki (ḥizqî, ‘my strength’ or ‘YHWH is strength’) is a shortened form of Hezekiah.
18: Ishmerai, Izliah, and Jobab close the sons of Elpaal. This section (vv17–18) provides nine sons for Elpaal across two verses, consistent with the Benjaminite pattern of large family clusters. Jobab appears here for the second time in 1 Chronicles 8 (also v9), confirming its currency as a Benjaminite name at multiple genealogical levels.
19: Jakim, Zichri, and Zabdi are “sons of Shimei.” This Shimei is not identified further; multiple Shimeis appear in Chronicles and the surrounding biblical narrative (most famously David’s curser, 2 Samuel 16:5–13). The Benjaminite Shimei sub-clans here have no direct narrative connection to the famous Shimei. Zichri (ziḵrî, ‘my memorial’ or from zāḵar, ‘to remember’) is a name asserting the parent’s hope that the child will be remembered — ironically appropriate for a genealogical register.
20: Elienai, Zillethai, and Eliel are the second trio of Shimei’s sons. Elienai (‘my eyes are toward God’) and Eliel (‘God is my God’ or ‘God, O God’) are theophoric names expressing devotion to El/God. The density of ʾēl-element names in this section (Michael v16, Elienai, Eliel) reflects a post-exilic naming fashion that the Chronicler has recorded without comment.
21: Adaiah, Beraiah, and Shimrath close Shimei’s sons. Adaiah (ʿăḏāyāh, ‘YHWH has adorned/testified’) is a priestly name common in Ezra-Nehemiah (Ezra 10:29, 39; Nehemiah 11:5, 12). Its appearance here in a Benjaminite warrior genealogy shows that names popular in the Levitical-priestly sphere also circulated in the lay tribal register. Shimrath (šimrāt, ‘guarding’) uses the same root as Shemer/Shamer, recurring Benjaminite-Asherite names.
22: Ishpan, Eber, and Eliel are sons of Shashak. Eber (ʿēḇer, the ancestral eponym of the Hebrews in Genesis 10:21–25) appears here as a Benjaminite personal name; the same name occurs in Elpaal’s list (v12). The repetition within 1 Chronicles 8 of common names (Eber, Gera, Zichri, Eliel) across different sub-clans reflects either actual naming practice or the possibility that similar names in the sources were assigned to different branches in the compilation.
23: Abdon, Zichri, and Hanan — sons of Shashak (continuing the list from v22). Abdon is a judge’s name (Judges 12:13–15) and a Levitical city in Asher (Joshua 21:30); its appearance as a Benjaminite sub-clan head underscores that judge-names circulated across tribal boundaries. Hanan (ḥānān, ‘gracious’) is a common shortened form of various theophoric names (Hannah, Hananiah).
24: Hananiah, Elam, and Antothijah close Shashak’s eleven sons (vv22–25). Hananiah (ḥananyāh, ‘YHWH is gracious’) is one of the most common theophoric names in the Hebrew Bible and appears prominently in the Babylonian captivity narrative (the Hebrew name of Shadrach, Daniel 1:6). Antothijah (‘answer of YHWH’ or ‘supplications of YHWH’) is a unique name in the Bible, possibly reflecting a family tradition of naming a child born in response to prayer.
25: Iphedeiah and Penuel close Shashak’s sub-list. Penuel (pᵉnûʾēl, ‘face of God’) is the name Jacob gave to the site of his wrestling match with the angel (Genesis 32:30); its use as a personal name takes the theophanic place-name into the private sphere. The two-name sub-list marks the end of Shashak’s documented line, with eleven sons recorded in total across vv22–25.
26: Shamsherai, Shehariah, and Athaliah are sons of Jeroham. Athaliah (ʿatalyhāh) is best known as the name of the queen who seized Judahite power after Ahaziah’s death (2 Kgs 11). Its appearance here as a masculine Benjaminite name is striking: the name was evidently gender-neutral, and the queen of Judah was named after a Benjaminite male ancestor. The name may derive from a root meaning ‘YHWH is exalted’ or ‘afflicted of YHWH.’
27: Jaareshiah, Elijah, and Zichri close Jeroham’s line. Elijah (ʾēliyyāh, ‘YHWH is my God’) is the same name as the Tishbite prophet (1 Kings 17:1). Its use as a Benjaminite personal name confirms that the name predated the famous prophet and was not an honorific coined for him. The three-name closing formula for Jeroham’s sons echoes the three-name pattern used throughout this section of 1 Chronicles 8.
28: “These were heads of ancestral houses throughout their generations — leading men who lived in Jerusalem.” The summary verse bridges the genealogical register to its geographic implication: these Benjaminite families are the established Jerusalem residents. The post-exilic context gives this note urgency: who has the legitimate claim to live in Jerusalem? The Chronicler answers by tracing Benjaminite Jerusalem residence to deep genealogical roots. Nehemiah 11:7–9 provides a parallel list of Benjaminites in Jerusalem, confirming this demographic claim.
29: “The father of Gibeon (ʾăḇî giḇʿôn) lived at Gibeon; his wife’s name was Maachah.” The formula “father of [city-name]” designates the clan-founder whose descendants settled and controlled that city. Gibeon is the Benjaminite town famous for the covenant with Joshua (Josh 9), the Gibeonite massacre under Saul (2 Samuel 21:1–9), and Solomon’s dream (1 Kings 3:4–15). Its connection to the Saul genealogy that follows is direct: Saul’s family was rooted in Gibeon, which explains Saul’s culpability in the Gibeonite massacre.
30: Gibeon’s patriarch’s firstborn son was Abdon, followed by Zur, Kish, Baal, and Nadab. Kish will become Saul’s father (v33) — here Kish is listed third among five brothers, establishing the modesty of Saul’s pedigree (not from the firstborn line). Baal as a personal name in a YHWH-worshipping Israelite family poses the same interpretive challenge as Eshbaal in v33: the word baʿal means ‘master/lord’ and was not necessarily polytheistic in early Israelite usage (cf. Hosea 2:16–17).
31: Gedor, Ahio, and Zacher are additional sons of the Gibeon patriarch. Gedor as a personal name echoes the Judahite place-name Gedor (1 Chronicles 4:4, 18); again, geographic and personal names overlap. Ahio appears in 2 Samuel 6:3–4 as one of the men who drove the ark-cart from Kirjath-jearim; whether this is the same Ahio or a coincidence of name is uncertain. Zacher is a shortened form of Zechariah (zᵉḵaryāh, ‘YHWH remembers’).
32: Mikloth fathered Shimeah (šimʿāh, ‘YHWH has heard’); both “also lived in Jerusalem, across from their kinsmen.” The note of dual residence (Gibeon for the main family; Jerusalem for the Mikloth-Shimeah branch) reflects the reality that aristocratic families in tribal societies maintained households in multiple locations. The phrase “across from their kinsmen” (negeḏ ʾᵃḥêhem) suggests proximity without subordination: a branch family maintaining its identity in Jerusalem while acknowledging connection to the Gibeonite parent clan.
34: Jonathan’s son was Meribbaal (mᵉrîḇ baʿal, ‘Baal contends’ or ‘Baal pleads [the cause]’); Meribbaal fathered Micah. The parallel in 2 Sam 9 and 2 Samuel 4:4 uses the Samuel editor’s substituted form Mephibosheth (mᵉpîḇōšet). Micah, Jonathan’s grandson, receives an extended genealogy in vv35–38, confirming that the Saul-Jonathan line continued into the post-monarchic period as a recognized Benjaminite family. The Chronicler’s interest in preserving this line may reflect a post-exilic Saulide community that appealed to it for tribal standing.
35: Micah’s four sons: Pithon, Melech, Tarea, and Ahaz. The naming of Micah’s son Ahaz is notable: Ahaz is the Davidic king who later brought Judah into crisis by his Assyrian alliances and altar-substitution (2 Chr 28). The Benjaminite Ahaz here precedes the Davidic Ahaz chronologically and is a different individual; the coincidence of names across tribal lines underscores that personal names in this period were not uniquely assigned.
36: Ahaz → Jehoadah → Alemeth, Azmaveth, Zimri; Zimri → Moza. Azmaveth (ʿazmāwet, ‘death is strong’ or ‘strong as death’) appears as a warrior in David’s army (2 Samuel 23:31) and as a place-name near Jerusalem (Nehemiah 12:29); the name’s military-mortuary meaning reflects the warrior ethos of the genealogy. Zimri is the name of the notorious Israelite king who killed Baasha’s line and reigned for seven days (1 Kings 16:9–20); the name was pre-existent as a personal name, not coined for the king.
37: Moza → Binea → Rapha → Eleasah → Azel. The deep genealogy of the Meribbaal-Micah line (six additional generations after Jonathan) anchors the Saulide family in a continuous genealogical record stretching from the Gibeon patriarch (v29) through Saul’s grandchildren and beyond. The Chronicler’s interest in this detail reflects either a living Saulide community in the post-exilic period or an archival fidelity to complete whatever genealogical sources he had.
38: Azel’s six sons: Azrikam, Bocheru, Ishmael, Sheariah, Obadiah, and Hanan. The six sons “all sons of Azel” is an emphatic closing formula asserting that all six are legitimate, full sons and not step-children or servants. Obadiah (ʿōḇaḏyāh, ‘servant of YHWH’) and Ishmael (yišmāʿēʾl, ‘God hears’) are theophoric names attesting continued YHWH-devotion in the Saulide family well after the dynasty’s end.
39: Azel’s brother Eshek has three sons: Ulam (firstborn), Jeush (second), Eliphelet (third). The ordinal markers (firstborn, second, third) appear here and at the start of the chapter (v1–2), framing the whole with ordinal register at both ends. Eliphelet (ʾelyepelet, ‘God is deliverance/escape’) is a common name among David’s sons (2 Samuel 5:16) and warriors (2 Samuel 23:34), suggesting its currency across social levels in Benjaminite-Judahite society.
40: Ulam’s sons are “mighty warriors, skilled archers (dōrᵉkê qešet), with many sons and grandsons — one hundred and fifty in all.” Benjamin was the tribe of the skilled bowman (Judges 20:16: 700 left-handed slingers; 1 Chronicles 12:2: Benjaminite ambidextrous archers who joined David). The final note “all these were of the sons of Benjamin” closes 1 Chronicles 8 and the entire two-chapter tribal survey (1 Chronicles 7–8) by re-anchoring the extended Saulide genealogy in its tribal identity. The closing formula “one hundred and fifty” descendants for Ulam alone demonstrates the Saulide family’s demographic vitality into the post-exilic period.
